proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the 80 binary features, at least one shows a clinically meaningful difference in objective_response rate between feature-positive and feature-negative patients (chi-square p < 0.001).
Among the 35 continuous features, at least one shows a difference in mean value between responders and non-responders (Welch t-test p < 0.001), indicating prognostic value.

Among the 10 multi-category features (feature_057, feature_086, feature_096, feature_064, feature_018, feature_045, feature_042, feature_125, feature_005, feature_087), at least one stratifies objective_response rate (chi-square p < 0.001).

analyses.code: for c in binary_cols: chi2, p = scipy.stats.chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[c], df['objective_response']))[:2]
analyses.result_summary: Across 80 binary features, the strongest univariate signal was feature_035 (prevalence 7.2%, n=3624): response rate 22.1% vs 16.5% in feature_035-negative patients (difference +5.6 pp; chi-square p=3.5e-18). feature_093 (RR 18.5% vs 16.8%, p=5.6e-3) and feature_121 (RR 15.6% vs 17.0%, p=0.025) reached p<0.05; most other binaries had p>0.05.

analyses.code: for c in continuous_cols: t, p = scipy.stats.ttest_ind(df.loc[y==1,c], df.loc[y==0,c], equal_var=False)
analyses.result_summary: Strongest continuous predictors: feature_011 (responder mean 3.38 vs non-responder 3.95, diff -0.57, p=7.2e-39); feature_006 (58.2 vs 60.3, diff -2.08, p=9.5e-20); feature_063 (5.65 vs 6.17, diff -0.51, p=4.4e-7); feature_099 (3.82 vs 3.80, diff +0.029, p=1.3e-6); feature_092 (19.1 vs 20.4, diff -1.33, p=1.3e-5). Higher values of feature_011, feature_006, feature_063, and feature_092 predict NON-response; higher values of feature_099 predict response.

analyses.code: for c in multicat_cols: stats.chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[c], y))
analyses.result_summary: feature_057 (3-level, ordinal-appearing) is the dominant categorical signal: response rate 21.3% (level 0) vs 15.5% (level 1) vs 11.2% (level 2), chi-square p=5.5e-98. feature_005 (race; 5 levels) shows a smaller signal: white 17.3%, hispanic 16.7%, asian 16.3%, black 15.7%, other 14.7% (p=0.0096). Other multi-category features (feature_086, feature_087=insurance, feature_096, feature_018, feature_045, feature_042, feature_064, feature_125) showed p>0.07.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher values of feature_057 are associated with lower odds of objective_response in an ordinal-linear fashion (logistic regression OR per level < 1, p < 0.001).

After mutual adjustment in a multivariable logistic model, feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_035, feature_099, feature_063, and feature_092 each retain independent associations with objective_response (each adjusted p < 0.01).
Patient age (feature_078) is independently associated with objective_response after adjusting for the top clinical predictors (adjusted p < 0.05).

analyses.result_summary: Ordinal logistic regression of objective_response on feature_057 (treated as 0/1/2): beta=-0.384 per level, OR=0.681 per level, z=-20.9, p=1.8e-97. Direction confirmed: each step up in feature_057 reduces response odds by ~32%.

All seven candidate predictors retained independent significance: feature_057 beta=-0.384 (p<1e-95); feature_011 beta=-0.040 (p<1e-33); feature_006 beta=-0.0057 (p<1e-19); feature_035 beta=+0.360 (p<1e-16, OR=1.43); feature_099 beta=+0.118 (p=1.1e-6); feature_063 beta=-0.0073 (p=1.4e-6); feature_092 beta=-0.0022 (p=9.6e-6). Pseudo-R^2 = 0.019.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(...).fit() with feature_078 in adjusted model
analyses.result_summary: Age (feature_078): adjusted beta=+0.0003, p=0.764. Quartile-stratified response rates were 16.7%/17.4%/16.6%/16.9% (q1-q4), all overlapping. Hypothesis NOT supported: age is not independently associated with objective_response in this cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_057 has differential effect on objective_response across feature_035 strata: the negative slope of feature_057 is steeper in feature_035-positive patients than in feature_035-negative patients (interaction p < 0.05).
feature_057 has differential effect on objective_response across feature_011 strata (interaction p < 0.05).
feature_057 has differential effect on objective_response across feature_006 strata (interaction p < 0.05).

analyses.result_summary: Interaction term feature_057 x feature_035: beta=+0.027, SE=0.064, p=0.674. NOT significant. feature_035's positive effect is approximately constant across feature_057 strata; main effects of feature_057 (beta=-0.385, p<1e-89) and feature_035 (beta=+0.340, p<1e-7) dominate.
Interaction feature_057 x feature_011: beta=-0.0019, p=0.710. NOT significant. feature_011's negative effect is similar across feature_057 strata.
Interaction feature_057 x feature_006: beta=-0.0009, p=0.330. NOT significant. feature_006's effect is approximately constant across feature_057 risk strata.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_035 (prevalence 7.2%) is associated with higher objective_response rate within every level of feature_057 (consistent positive effect across risk strata).

Adjusting for feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_099, feature_063, feature_092 (the dominant prognostic predictors), feature_035 retains a positive independent association with objective_response (adjusted OR > 1, p < 0.001).

analyses.result_summary: feature_035 raises response rate within every feature_057 stratum: feature_057=0: 27.0% (f35+) vs 20.8% (f35-), diff +6.2pp; feature_057=1: 20.5% vs 15.1%, diff +5.4pp; feature_057=2: 15.3% vs 10.9%, diff +4.4pp. Consistent positive direction, consistent magnitude (~5pp), supporting hypothesis.

Adjusted feature_035 coefficient: beta=+0.357, SE=0.042, OR=1.43, p=2.9e-17. feature_035 is independently associated with higher objective_response after controlling for the dominant clinical predictors. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Race (feature_005) is associated with objective_response at the unadjusted level, with white patients having the highest response rate and other/black patients the lowest (chi-square p < 0.05).

Race-based differences in objective_response are explained by differential distribution of clinical predictors (feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_035) across racial groups; after adjustment, no race coefficient differs from white at p < 0.05.
Insurance type (feature_087: medicare, private, medicaid, uninsured) is associated with objective_response (unadjusted chi-square p < 0.05).
feature_035's effect on objective_response differs in magnitude between white and black patients (interaction p < 0.05).

analyses.result_summary: Unadjusted response rates: white 17.28%, hispanic 16.70%, asian 16.33%, black 15.74%, other 14.72%. Chi-square p=0.0096. White-vs-black gap = +1.54 pp. Hypothesis SUPPORTED at unadjusted level, although effect sizes are small.

After adjustment for the top clinical predictors, race coefficients (vs reference 'asian'): black beta=-0.030 p=0.63; hispanic beta=+0.045 p=0.45; other beta=-0.117 p=0.25; white beta=+0.080 p=0.13. NO race effect reaches p<0.05 after adjustment. The unadjusted gap is explained by clinical confounding (e.g., black patients have slightly higher mean feature_011, the strongest negative predictor: 3.97 vs 3.82 in white). Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

Insurance: medicare 17.18%, medicaid 16.87%, private 16.66%, uninsured 16.62%. Chi-square p=0.54. NO association with objective_response, neither unadjusted nor adjusted (all coefficients p>0.55). Hypothesis NOT supported.

analyses.code: stratified logistic by race; sm.Logit(y_race, X[feature_035])
analyses.result_summary: feature_035 effect within race subgroups: white n=32,498 beta=+0.406 p=2.6e-15; black n=5,889 beta=+0.285 p=0.025; hispanic n=7,631 beta similar in direction. The point estimates differ slightly but the formal interaction (Wald test in pooled model with race x feature_035 product term) was not significant (p>0.10). Direction of feature_035's positive effect is consistent across racial groups. Hypothesis NOT supported (no statistically reliable race differential).

proposed_hypotheses.text: The relationship between feature_011 and objective_response is monotonic but possibly non-linear; including a quadratic term improves fit (likelihood-ratio p < 0.05).
feature_092's relationship with objective_response is non-linear (LR p<0.05 for quadratic term).
Patients in the lowest quintile of feature_011 have at least 50% higher response rate (relative) than patients in the highest quintile.

analyses.code: LR test: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(['lin','sq'])).fit().llf vs linear-only model
analyses.result_summary: feature_011 quadratic term beta=-0.0014, LR p=0.070 - borderline non-significant. Linear model adequate.

feature_092: linear beta=-0.0022, quadratic beta=+1.18e-5, LR p=8.3e-4. Mild U-shape supported, although effect is small and direction is dominated by the negative linear term across the bulk of the distribution.

feature_011 quintiles (low to high): RR 18.6%, 17.6%, 16.3%, 13.3% (only 4 quantile groups - data has many ties at the lower bound). Lowest-vs-highest RR ratio = 18.6/13.3 = 1.40 (40% higher), short of the predicted 50%. Hypothesis NOT supported in magnitude, but the direction (lower feature_011 = higher response) is confirmed.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the top 10 predictors (feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_035, feature_099, feature_063, feature_092, feature_093, feature_014, feature_121), at least one pairwise interaction term is significant at p<0.001 in a 3-parameter logistic model.
feature_035 and feature_014 show a positive multiplicative interaction: feature_014's effect on objective_response is larger in feature_035-positive patients than in feature_035-negative patients.

analyses.code: for each pair (a,b) in top10: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant([a,b,a*b])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Top pairwise interactions: feature_035 x feature_014 beta=+0.730 p=7.7e-18; feature_093 x feature_121 beta=+0.421 p=3.7e-3; feature_006 x feature_092 beta=-7.2e-5 p=5.6e-3; feature_011 x feature_121 beta=-0.034 p=7.7e-3; feature_099 x feature_121 beta=+0.221 p=0.013. Hypothesis SUPPORTED: feature_035 x feature_014 is highly significant.

2x2 cell rates: f014=0/f035=0 RR=16.6% (n=27,791); f014=0/f035=1 RR=17.1% (n=2,189); f014=1/f035=0 RR=16.4% (n=18,585); f014=1/f035=1 RR=29.8% (n=1,435). feature_014 has no effect when feature_035-negative (16.4 vs 16.6, p=0.62) but doubles response rate when feature_035-positive (29.8 vs 17.1, OR=2.05, p=9e-19). Strong qualitative interaction. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Across all 3,038 binary-binary interaction tests, additional significant interactions involving feature_035 with other binary features exist beyond feature_035 x feature_014 (Bonferroni-significant at p<0.05/3038 ~= 1.6e-5).
feature_014, feature_027, and feature_035 show a synergistic three-way interaction: response rate is dramatically elevated only when all three are positive simultaneously.

The three-way interaction feature_014 x feature_027 x feature_035 retains significance after adjusting for all main effects, all two-way interactions, and the dominant clinical predictors (feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_099, feature_063, feature_092).

analyses.code: for each (a,b) in binary_cols choose 2: sm.Logit(y, [a,b,a*b]).fit() - 3038 tests
analyses.result_summary: Bonferroni-significant binary x binary interactions (p<1.6e-5): only three pairs, ALL involving the same triplet: feature_035 x feature_027 (beta=+1.046, p=1.1e-32); feature_035 x feature_014 (beta=+0.730, p=7.7e-18); feature_027 x feature_014 (beta=+0.261, p=6.6e-7). No other binary-pair interaction reaches Bonferroni significance. Hypothesis SUPPORTED: feature_035 x feature_027 is the strongest pairwise interaction.

2x2x2 cell rates: 7 of the 8 cells show RR=15.9%-17.3%, but the cell with feature_014=1 AND feature_027=1 AND feature_035=1 has RR=62.06% (n=427, 265 responders). Logistic regression with full main+2-way+3-way decomposition: 3-way coefficient beta=+2.196, SE=0.193, z=+11.4, p<1e-29. Likelihood-ratio test for the 3-way term: chi-square=137.4, p<1e-31. Hypothesis STRONGLY SUPPORTED.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant([all_main, c14_27, c14_35, c27_35, c14_27_35, feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_099, feature_063, feature_092])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: After full adjustment (all main effects, all 2-way interactions, and 6 dominant prognostic covariates), the three-way coefficient feature_014 x feature_027 x feature_035 is beta=+2.196, p<1e-29. The 7 'non-triple-positive' cells have model-predicted RR ~15-17%; the triple-positive cell has predicted RR ~62%. Hypothesis SUPPORTED. Pseudo-R^2 of full model = 0.0275.

proposed_hypotheses.text: In the fully adjusted model containing all main effects, the three-way interaction, and demographic covariates, the dominant clinical predictors (feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_099, feature_063, feature_092) all retain p<0.001.
In the fully adjusted model, no demographic covariate (race, insurance, age, feature_007) reaches p<0.05.

Patients positive for the triple feature_014/feature_027/feature_035 have substantially higher absolute response rates than the overall cohort (~17%); the absolute treatment effect compared to the rest of the cohort is at least +30 percentage points.

analyses.result_summary: Final model coefficients (all p-values are Wald): feature_057 beta=-0.390 p<1e-95; feature_011 beta=-0.041 p<1e-34; feature_006 beta=-0.0059 p<1e-19; feature_099 beta=+0.124 p=3.2e-7; feature_063 beta=-0.0075 p=6.7e-7; feature_092 beta=-0.0022 p=2.0e-5. All six retain p<0.001 even with the 3-way interaction in the model. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

Demographics in fully adjusted model: race_white p=0.16, race_black p=0.54, race_hispanic p=0.55, race_other p=0.32; ins_medicare p=0.56, ins_private p=0.68, ins_uninsured p=0.94; feature_078 (age) p=0.79; feature_007 p=0.28. NO demographic covariate reaches p<0.05 after clinical adjustment. Hypothesis SUPPORTED.

Triple-positive RR = 62.06% (n=427, 265/427 responders). All other patients pooled (n=49,573): RR = 16.51% (8187/49573). Absolute difference = +45.55 percentage points. Hypothesis SUPPORTED with substantial margin (predicted >30pp). This subgroup represents 0.85% of the cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The pattern of effects (modest gradient by feature_057, individual contributions of feature_011/feature_006/feature_099/feature_063/feature_092, and the dominant feature_014 x feature_027 x feature_035 synergy with no race/insurance/age effects after adjustment) is consistent with: (a) feature_057 being a prognostic risk-stratification variable; (b) feature_011 a pre-treatment laboratory marker (e.g., bilirubin/blast burden); (c) feature_035 a predictive biomarker; (d) feature_014 and feature_027 two treatments whose joint efficacy is unlocked by the biomarker.

Restricting analysis to patients with feature_035=1 (n=3,624) preserves the three-way pattern: the joint effect of feature_014 + feature_027 lifts response rate above the feature_035-positive baseline.

analyses.result_summary: All findings consistent with the integrated narrative: a single prognostic categorical (feature_057, OR 0.68/level), four prognostic continuous lab-like markers (feature_011 negative, feature_099 positive, feature_063 negative, feature_092 negative), a constitutional negative continuous (feature_006), a predictive biomarker (feature_035), and two treatments (feature_014, feature_027) whose synergy with the biomarker drives a 45pp lift in response rate. Demographics (race, insurance, age, feature_007) confer no independent association after clinical adjustment.

Within the n=3,624 feature_035-positive subset, response rates are: f014=0/f027=0 RR=17.27% (n=1,569); f014=0/f027=1 RR=16.77% (n=620); f014=1/f027=0 RR=16.07% (n=1,008); f014=1/f027=1 RR=62.06% (n=427). Two-way interaction within feature_035-positive: beta=+2.18, p<1e-65 (Fisher exact OR=8.5). Hypothesis SUPPORTED: triple positivity is required - neither f014 alone nor f027 alone produces a response benefit even in biomarker-positive patients.
